ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers corn-based snack chips; no specific nachos-with-cheese code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for cheese-loaded nachos as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: cheddar cheese or processed cheese sauce
allergens: gluten (corn chips may be processed in shared facilities; cheese sauce may contain wheat thickener)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of cheese loaded nachos is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a shared plate of tortilla chips generously covered with melted cheese and assorted toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Nachos, cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 21110)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for nachos with cheese (NDB 21110 / FDC ID 172929) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent a standard cheese nacho preparation with tortilla chips and cheese topping. The restaurant version served in Singapore may include additional toppings (jalapeños, sour cream, salsa, beans) which can modestly alter sodium, fat, and fibre values; core macros are taken directly from the USDA entry and are considered representative.

gi_source.title: Corn chips, plain, salted — Glycemic Index Database; and tortilla/corn chip GI reference values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese-loaded nachos as a composite dish. The GI of 63 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain corn chips / tortilla chips (GI approximately 63, medium category), which form the dominant glycemic carbohydrate component of this dish. The cheese and fat toppings reduce the overall glycemic response relative to plain chips; however, the chip base GI is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (63 × 100.5) / 100 ≈ 63.4, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 33.5 × 3 = 100.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (480 mg per 100 g; approximately 1440 mg per standard 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid cheese-loaded nachos due to the very high sodium load from processed cheese, salted tortilla chips, and condiments, which can significantly elevate blood pressure; a single serving may provide more than 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate content and medium-to-high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume cheese-loaded nachos only occasionally and in small portions, as the high carbohydrate content from refined corn tortilla chips (33.5 g per 100 g) combined with a medium GI of 63 produces a high glycemic load per standard serving (~63 g GL), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (5.8 g per 100 g), high sodium, and high energy density (306 kcal per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat cheese-loaded nachos as an occasional indulgence only, as the combination of saturated fat from cheese, high sodium from chips and condiments, and the overall high caloric density collectively contribute to dyslipidaemia risk, endothelial stress, and weight gain, all of which are adverse for cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese Loaded Nachos is a popular Tex-Mex snack dish consisting of crispy corn tortilla chips generously topped with melted cheese — typically cheddar or a processed cheese sauce — along with a variety of zesty accompaniments such as jalapeño peppers, sour cream, fresh salsa, diced tomatoes, black beans, and spring onions. The dish is served hot, usually as a shared starter or snack, and is characterised by its combination of crunchy, salty chips with rich, creamy, and tangy toppings. Originating in the Tex-Mex culinary tradition of the United States–Mexico border region, nachos have become a globally recognised comfort food and are widely available in Mexican-themed restaurants in Singapore, including at establishments such as Fiesta Mexico and Three Amigos. Nutritionally, cheese-loaded nachos are energy-dense at approximately 306 kcal per 100 g, with a significant fat contribution (16.8 g per 100 g, of which 5.8 g is saturated) from the cheese component, and a substantial carbohydrate load (33.5 g per 100 g) from the corn chips. Sodium content is high at 480 mg per 100 g, making a standard 300 g restaurant serving a significant source of dietary sodium (~1440 mg). The glycemic index is estimated at 63 (medium), driven primarily by the refined corn chip base, and the glycemic load per serving is high. Clinically, this dish warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and glycemic load profile. It is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans, and is not inherently gluten-free or halal-certified.
common_user_queries: What are the calories in cheese nachos?
How much sodium is in nachos?
What is the GI of nachos?
Are nachos high in saturated fat?
Nachos for hypertension — is it safe?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for nachos with cheese (FDC ID 172929), which is an approved secondary global source. No Singapore-specific or HPB Foodo entry exists for this dish. The USDA entry is a reasonable proxy for the cheese nacho base, but restaurant versions in Singapore may vary in topping composition, portion size, and cheese type. GI is estimated from the analogous corn chip entry in the University of Sydney GI Database rather than a direct measurement of this composite dish. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172929, nachos with cheese). No HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this dish. USDA is the highest-priority approved source available for this Western/Tex-Mex food item. 2. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain corn/tortilla chips; no direct GI measurement for cheese-loaded nachos as a composite dish was found in the approved GI source database. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are reported from the USDA source where available. 5. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Snacks', as nachos are a tortilla-chip-based snack dish; dairy is a component ingredient rather than the primary food group classification. 6. Halal status is listed as false as the dish is served at a restaurant that also serves Indian food and halal certification cannot be confirmed from available data. 7. The input cuisine_tag 'Naan' is not relevant to this dish and has been excluded from the output record. 8. All -1 sentinel values represent nutrients not reported in the cited USDA source for this food entry.
